Asked, "The Tripitaka Master Great-Ear searched for the National Teacher a third time and did not see him. Where, then, was the National Teacher
?" The master said, "Inside the Tripitaka Master's nostrils."
Asked, "What about when a blind turtle meets the hole in a floating log?" The master said, "That is no accidental matter."
Asked, "What if one dwells long in cliffs and valleys?" The master said, "Why not hide away?"

Asked, "What is the great meaning of the Buddha Dharma?" The master said, "Bow." As the monk was about to continue speaking, the master called
the novice Wenyuan. Wenyuan came, and the master shouted, "Where have you just come from?"
Asked, "What is one's own original intention?" The master said, "This old monk has no need of an ox-cleaver."

Asked, "For long I have heard of Zhaozhou's stone bridge, but on arriving I see only a log bridge." The master said, "Venerable, you only see
the log bridge and do not see Zhaozhou's stone bridge?" He said, "What is Zhaozhou's stone bridge?" The master said, "Cross
over, come across."

Another monk asked, "For long I have heard of Zhaozhou's stone bridge, but on arriving I see only a log bridge." The master said, "You only
see the log bridge and do not see Zhaozhou's stone bridge?" He said, "What is the stone bridge?" The master said, "It carries donkeys
and carries horses."

Asked, "Master, what is your surname?" The master said, "Changzhou has it." He said, "How many years of the cycle are you?" The master said, "Suzhou
has it."

The master ascended the hall and said, "As soon as there is right and wrong, the mind is lost in confusion. Is there still any part for answering words?" There was
a monk who came out and struck the attendant once, saying, "Why do you not answer the master?" The master then returned to the abbot's quarters. Later
the attendant asked for instruction, "Did that monk just now understand or not?" The master said, "The one sitting sees the one standing; the one standing
sees the one sitting."

Asked, "What is the Way?" The master said, "The one outside the wall." He said, "I am not asking about that." The master said, "What Way are you asking
about?" He said, "The Great Way." The master said, "The Great Way leads to Chang'an."
Asked, "What about sweeping away dust and seeing Buddha?" The master said, "Sweeping away dust is not absent, but seeing Buddha cannot be attained."
Asked, "What is the body without illness?" The master said, "The four elements and the five skandhas."

Asked, "What is an icchantika?" The master said, "Why not ask about bodhi?" He said, "What is bodhi?" The master
said, "Just this is the icchantika."

Sometimes the master curled his fingers and said, "This old monk calls it a fist. What do you all call it?" A monk said,
"How can the master present an object to people?" The master said, "I do not present objects to people. If I were to present an object
to you, Venerable, I would bury you." He said, "What about this then?" The master at once said farewell.

Asked, "One question and one answer always fall into heavenly demons and outsiders; but if one remains silent, one also falls into his trap and net.
What is Zhaozhou's family style?" The master said, "You do not know how to ask." He said, "Please answer, Master." The master
said, "According to your case, you deserve twenty blows."

The master addressed the assembly, saying, "As soon as there is right and wrong, the mind is lost in confusion. Is there still any part for answering words?" There was
a monk who came out, slapped the novice once, and then left. The master returned to the abbot's quarters. The next day he asked
the attendant, "Where is that monk from yesterday?" The attendant said, "He left right then." The master
said, "For thirty years I played at riding horses, and got thrown by a donkey."

Asked, "If someone comes in such a way, does the master receive him or not?" The master said, "I receive him." He said, "If someone does not come in such a way,
does the master receive him or not?" The master said, "I receive him." He said, "One who comes in such a way is received by the master; one who does not come in such a way,
how does the master receive him?" The master said, "Stop, stop, no need to speak. My Dharma is subtle and hard to conceive."

The King of Zhen Prefecture asked, "Reverend Master, at your age, how many teeth remain?" The master said, "Only one tooth."
The king said, "How can you eat things?" The master said, "Though only one, every bite catches."

Asked, "What is the student's jewel?" The master said, "Ask loudly." The monk bowed. The master said, "You do not know how to ask.
Why not say, 'I do not ask about high or low; what is the student's jewel?' Why not ask like that?" The monk
then asked again. The master said, "I should have let that fellow go."
Asked, "When the two sides are silent and still, how does the master expound?" The master said, "This year there are no storms."

Asked, "With the whole assembly gathered like clouds, what should be discussed?" The master said, "Today we drag timbers and raise the monks' hall." He said,
"Could it be that just this is receiving students?" The master said, "This old monk does not know backgammon, nor
does he know long rows."

Asked, "What is the true human body?" The master said, "Spring, summer, autumn, winter." He said, "Then the student
finds it hard to understand?" The master said, "You asked me about the true human body."

Asked, "What is the great meaning of the Buddha Dharma?" The master said, "What is your name?" He said, "So-and-so." The master said, "In the Hanyuan Hall,
in the Golden Valley Garden."
Asked, "Who is the teacher of the seven Buddhas?" The master said, "When wanting to sleep, sleep; when wanting to rise, rise."

Asked, "The Way is not outside things, and outside things is not the Way. What is the Way outside things?" The master struck him. He said, "master,
do not hit me. Later on you may hit the wrong person." The master said, "Dragons and snakes are easy to distinguish, but patch-robed monks
are hard to fool."

When the master saw the king enter the monastery, he did not rise, but slapped his own knee with his hand and said, "Do you understand?" The king said, "I do not
understand." The master said, "I left home young and now am old; when I see people, I lack the strength to get down from the Chan couch."
Asked, "What is honest speech?" The master said, "Your mother is ugly."

Asked, "What about one who from the past until now does not forget?" The master said, "Do not bind the mind to what cannot be attained; always think on
and recollect all Buddhas of the ten directions."
Asked, "What is honest speech?" The master said, "Eat an iron staff."
Asked, "What is the matter above Buddha?" The master clapped his hands and laughed loudly.

Asked, "One lamp lights hundreds and thousands of lamps; from where does the first lamp issue?" The master kicked out
one sandal, and then said, "A real adept would not ask like that."

Asked, "Returning to the root one gets the point, but following illumination one loses the source. What then?" The master said, "This old monk does not answer that question."
He said, "Please answer, Master." The master said, "Should it be like this?"
Asked, "What is the place of no thinking?" The master said, "Speak quickly, speak quickly."

Asked, "Ascending Tusita at night and descending Jambudvipa by day, why does the mani jewel not appear therein?" The master said,
"What are you saying?" The monk asked again. The master said, "Vipashin Buddha set his mind on this early, and right up to now has not
attained the marvel."
Asked, "What about the place beyond thought?" The master said, "Speak at once, speak at once."

Asked, "What is the jewel in the robe?" The master said, "What fault is there with this question?" He said, "This is the question;
what is the jewel?" The master said, "If so, then even the robe is lost."
Asked, "What when for ten thousand miles there is no inn?" The master said, "Lodge in the Chan monastery."
Asked, "Does a dog still have Buddha-nature or not?" The master said, "In front of every house gate the road leads to Chang'an."

Asked, "If presented face to face, does that fully express the great meaning or not?" The master said, "Lower your voice." He said, "What about where it cannot be taken in
?" The master said, "I tell you, lower your voice."
Asked, "What is the phrase right before us?" The master said, "This old monk is not as good as you."
Asked, "Who is the one that comes forth?" The master said, "Buddhas and bodhisattvas."

Asked, "What about before the numinous grass has sprouted?" The master said, "If you smell it, your skull splits." He said, "What if one does not smell it?"
The master said, "Like a dead man standing." He said, "Is the student still allowed to harmonize?" The master said, "When people come, do not
tell them."
Asked, "Are the patriarchs' meaning and the teachings' meaning the same or different?" The master said, "Just left home and not yet ordained, and already asking people everywhere."

Asked, "What is the holy?" The master said, "Not ordinary." He said, "What is ordinary?" The master said, "Not holy." He said, "When not
ordinary and not holy, then what?" The master said, "A fine Chan monk."
Asked, "When two mirrors face each other, which is brightest?" The master said, "Your eyelids, Venerable, cover Mount Sumeru."
Asked, "I have only recently entered the monasteries; I beg the master for instruction." The master said, "Blue heaven, blue heaven."

Asked, "What when the earlier phrase is gone and the later phrase is hard to make clear?" The master said, "The moment you name it, it will not do." He said,
"Please distinguish it, Master." The master said, "Ask, ask."
Asked, "What when it is steep and hard to climb?" The master said, "This old monk does not go to the summit of high peaks."
Asked, "Who is the one not companion to the ten thousand dharmas?" The master said, "Not a person."
Asked, "Please, Master, a phrase of the middle way in the school vehicle." The master said, "Today I have no money for the official."
Asked, "I will not ask differently; please, Master, do not answer differently." The master said, "Strange."

Asked, "Outside the teachings of the three vehicles, how do you receive people?" The master said, "Since this world came to be, the sun and moon have never
changed."
Asked, "When the three places do not connect, how does one leave consciousness?" The master said, "Consciousness is outside the matter."

Asked, "When all capacities gather together, what is the matter within?" The master said, "My eyes are originally straight; I do not speak
of the matter within."

Asked, "Who is the one who does not stop on pure ground?" The master said, "You are not yet among those people." He said, "Then
who is among them?" The master said, "Stop."

Asked, "What is the source of the ten thousand dharmas?" The master said, "Beams, rafters, and pillars." He said, "The student does not understand." The master
said, "Brackets and joined hands, and still you do not understand."
Asked, "What when not a single thing is brought along?" The master said, "Put it down."

Asked, "If on the road one meets a realized person, and one does not respond with speech or silence, with what should one respond?" The master said, "A person
comes from Chenzhou and does not get a letter from Xuzhou."

Asked, "Opening the mouth is conditioned action; what is unconditioned?" The master pointed with his hand and said, "This is
unconditioned." He said, "This is conditioned; what is unconditioned?" The master said, "Unconditioned." He said, "This
is conditioned." The master said, "It is conditioned."
The master addressed the assembly, saying, "The single word Buddha, I do not like to hear."
Asked, "Does the master still teach people or not?" The master said, "Buddha, Buddha."

Asked, "When the present moment is exhausted, what is the exact place?" The master said, "Exhaust the present moment, and do not ask about that
one." He said, "What is the exact?" The master said, "I tell you, do not ask." He said, "How can it be seen?" The master said,
"Great has no outside; small has no inside."

Asked, "What when one is beyond the four propositions and cuts off the hundred negations?" The master said, "This old monk does not recognize death." He said, "This
is the master's own matter." The master said, "Exactly so." He said, "Please give instruction, master." The master said, "Beyond
the four propositions and the hundred negations cut off, with what would I instruct you?"
Asked, "What is the master's family style?" The master said, "Inside, not one thing; outside, nothing sought."
Asked, "What is returning to the root and attaining the point?" The master said, "If I answer you, it misses."
Asked, "What is the mind of doubt?" The master said, "If I answer you, it also misses."

Asked, "Does one who has left home still become worldly or not?" The master said, "One who leaves home is at once a seat-master. Leave or not leave,
this old monk does not manage it." He said, "Why do you not manage it?" The master said, "Thus, that itself is leaving home."

Asked, "What about a disciple without a teacher?" The master said, "The undefiled wisdom-nature is originally complete." He also said,
"This is a disciple without a teacher."
Asked, "What when no boundary marks are seen?" The master said, "Why is it thus?"

Asked, "What when clear yet not pure, muddled yet not turbid?" The master said, "Not pure, not turbid." He said, "What
is that?" The master said, "Rather pitiful." He said, "What is mastery in all directions?" The master said, "Leave aside the Vajra
Chan."

A monk asked, What is the jewel in the bag? The master said, What is there to dislike? The monk said, When its use is inexhaustible, how is it? The master
said, Is your own one still weighty or not? He also said, When used it is heavy; when not used it is light.

A monk asked, What is the Patriarch's true meaning? The master coughed up phlegm and spat. The monk said, What about the matter within that? The master again
spat on the ground.
A monk asked, What is the conduct of a śramaṇa? The master said, Conduct free from conduct.
A monk asked, Please point out the place of true rest. The master said, If I point it out, it is not rest.
A monk asked, How is it when there is no question? The master said, It goes against ordinary speech.
A monk asked, How is it when the four mountains press in? The master said, There are no tracks of escape.

A monk asked, When one gets here and cannot speak, then what? The master said, It cannot be spoken. The monk said, How should one speak? The master
said, Speak from the place where it cannot be spoken.

A monk asked, As long as there are words and phrases, none go beyond the summit. What is the matter outside the summit? The master called the novice Wen-
yuan. Wenyuan answered, Yes? The master said, It is already late in the day.
A monk asked, What is Vairocana Buddha? The master said, Do not speak foully.

A monk asked, The supreme Way is not difficult, it only dislikes choosing and selecting. How can one avoid choosing and selecting? The master said, Above heaven and
below heaven, I alone am honored. The monk said, That is still choosing and selecting. The master said, Storehouse slave, where is
the choosing and selecting?
A monk asked, Who is a person outside the three realms? The master said, What can you do about this old monk being within the three realms?
A monk asked, What about one who knows there is and yet is not in being? The master said, If you ask further, you are deliberately questioning this old monk.

The master addressed the assembly, Head south and go to the monasteries; do not stay here. A monk then asked, Reverend,
what sort of place is here? The master said, My here is a firewood grove.
A monk asked, What is Vairocana Buddha? The master said, By nature, one is a disciple.

A monk asked, How is it when one returns to the root and gets the meaning? The master said, Too hasty. The monk said, I do not understand. The master said, Not
understanding starts from where?

Minister Liu entered the monastery and saw the master sweeping the ground. He asked, Great good teacher, why are you sweeping away dust?
The master said, It comes from outside.

A monk asked, How is it when a sharp sword leaves the scabbard? The master said, Dark. The monk said, As I ask directly, how can it be made clear?
The master said, I have no such idle time. The monk said, What if I fold my hands before others? The master said, Sooner or later
I will see you folding your hands. The monk said, How about when one is not folding hands? The master said, Who is the one not folding hands?
A monk asked, Where is the śramaṇa's place of power? The master said, Where are you not effective?

A monk asked, Where does the master instruct students? The master said, Right before me there are no students. The monk said, Then
that means you do not appear in the world. The master then said, Take care.

A monk asked, Are the Patriarch's meaning and the teachings' meaning the same or different? The master made a fist and placed it on his head. The monk said, Reverend, you still have this
thing there? The master took off his hat and said, Tell me, what does this old monk have?

A monk asked, How is it when the mind neither stops nor abides? The master said, It is a living thing. Right here, this very one is being
driven by mind-consciousness. The monk said, How can one avoid being driven by mind-consciousness? The master lowered his head.

A monk asked, From where does the Way arise? The master said, This arises, but the Way does not belong to arising and ceasing. The monk said, Could it be
natural or not? The master said, This is natural; the Way is not like that.
A monk asked, Are the Patriarch's meaning and the teachings' meaning the same or different? The master said, If you understand the Patriarch's meaning, then you understand the teachings' meaning.
A monk asked, What is moving among other kinds? The master said, Om lin, om lin.

A monk asked, How is it when the height is steep and hard to climb? The master said, This old monk lives on the peak itself. The monk said, What about Cao-
xi by the roadside? The master said, Caoxi is bad. The monk said, Why does no one get there nowadays? The master said,
It is because it is steep.
A monk asked, What is the jeweled moon in the empty sky? The master said, It stops up this old monk's ears.
A monk asked, What if there is the slightest deviation? The master said, Crude. The monk said, What about responding to the occasion? The master said, Bent.
A monk asked, What is the conduct of a śramaṇa? The master stretched out his hand and brushed his robe.
A monk asked, How is it where the life of Patriarchs and Buddhas does not cease? The master said, No one knows.
A monk asked, What is expedient function called? The master said, It is called expedient function.

A monk asked, This student has recently entered the monastery and does not understand. I beg the master for instruction. The master said, Before entering the monastery, even more
you did not understand.

A monk asked, What did the ancient worthies of old use to instruct people? The master said, If not for your question, this old monk would not even know
there were ancient worthies. The monk said, Please instruct me. The master said, This old monk is not an ancient worthy.

A monk asked, Before the Buddha-flower has opened, how does one discern the true and real? The master said, It is true, it is real. The monk said, On whose
account is this matter? The master said, This old monk has a share; you, reverend, have a share.
A monk asked, What is Buddha? The master said, What sort of person are you?
A monk asked, How is it on the straight road? The master said, The straight road.
A monk asked, What is the mystery within mystery, unbroken mystery? The master said, Your asking me is unbroken mystery.

A monk asked, Before the flower of awakening has opened, how does one discern the real? The master said, It has already opened. The monk said, I still do not know
whether it is true or real. The master said, True is real, real is true.

A monk asked, Is there anyone who does not repay the four kinds of grace and the three virtues? The master said, Yes. The monk said, Who is that? The master
said, A fellow who betrays grace and fails virtue.
A monk asked, When a poor son comes, what thing do you give him? The master said, Nothing is lacking.
A monk asked, Who is the true host of Zhaozhou? The master said, This old monk is Congshen.

An old woman asked, I am in a body burdened by the five obstructions; how can I be freed from them? The master said, May all people
be reborn in heaven; may you, old woman, sink forever in the bitter sea.

A monk asked, How is it when the bright moon is in the sky? The master said, You are still a fellow below the steps. The monk said, Please receive me up the
steps. The master said, When the moon has set, come see me.

At times the master addressed the assembly, When this old monk first arrived at Yaoshan, I got one phrase, and right up to now
I have been stuffed full, snoring with fullness.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Recorded Sayings of Reverend Zhaozhou, Lower Scroll, together with encounters, examinations, discriminations, verses, and hymns

cb:div.p:
Once while the master was seated in meditation in his room, the steward reported, Great King has come to pay respects. The king
bowed, and an attendant asked, When the ruler comes, why did you not rise? The master said, You do not understand.
Here with this old monk, when lower-grade people come, I go out to the main gate to receive them; when middle-grade people come, I get down from the meditation platform to receive them;
when upper-grade people come, I receive them on the meditation platform. One cannot call the Great King middle-grade or lower-grade,
for fear of slighting him. The king was delighted and repeatedly invited him inside for offerings.

The master asked Lay Official Zhou, Have you ever dreamed of Linji? The official raised his fist. The master
said, On that side did you see him? The official said, On this side I saw him. The master said, Where did you see Linji? The official had no
reply. The master asked, Where does Official Zhou come from? He said, Neither coming nor going. The master said, I am not an old
crow, flying here and there.

The master addressed the assembly, As soon as there is right and wrong, the mind is lost in confusion. Is there still any share in answering? Later
a monk cited this to Luopu, and Luopu clicked his teeth; he also cited it to Yunju, and Yunju said, Why bother?
The monk cited it to the master. The master said, In the south many people lose body and life. The monk said, Please, master,
give an example. As soon as the master raised it, the monk pointed to a monk beside him and said, This monk here, after eating his meal, what
talk is he making?

Once while the master was reading the Diamond Sutra, a monk asked, All Buddhas and the Buddhas' unsurpassed bodhi
come forth from this sutra. What is this sutra? The master said, Diamond Prajñā Pāramitā
Sutra, Thus have I heard: at one time the Buddha was in Śrāvastī. The monk said, No. The master said, I myself
may not read the sutra either?

When a monk was taking leave, the master said, When you go out, if someone suddenly asks, Have you seen Zhaozhou or not?
how will you reply? The monk said, I can only say I have. The master said, This old monk is a donkey; how will you
say you saw me? The monk had no reply.

The master asked a newly arrived monk, Where do you come from? He said, From the south. The master said, Do you know there is a Zhaozhou
barrier? He said, One must know the Zhaozhou barrier. The master shouted, You peddler of smuggled salt! Then he said, Brother,
the Zhaozhou barrier is hard to pass. The monk said, What is the Zhaozhou barrier? The master said, The stone bridge is.

A monk came from Xuefeng. The master said, Reverend, do not stay here. This old monk's place is only
a place to avoid trouble. The Buddha-Dharma is entirely in the south. The monk said, How can the Buddha-Dharma have north and south? The master said, Even if
you come from Yunju or Xuefeng, you are still just a plank-carrying fellow. The monk said, I do not yet know how things are over there.
The master said, Why did you wet the bed last night? The monk said, After realization, how is it? The master said,
Still shit.

He addressed the assembly, Here I have lions coming out of the cave, and also lions staying in the cave; it is only hard
to get a lion cub. At that time a monk snapped his fingers in reply. The master said, What is that? He said, A lion cub.
The master said, For me to call it a lion cub is already a fault, and you go on trampling further.

He asked a newly arrived monk, From what place did you depart? He said, From Xuefeng. The master said, What sayings does Xuefeng
use to instruct people? He said, The master often says, The whole world in the ten directions is the śramaṇa's one eye. You
people, where will you shit? The master said, If you return, send him a shovel.

While the master was distributing clothes to the assembly, a monk asked, master, after giving everything away, what will you
use? The master called, Fellow from Huzhou. The monk answered, Yes. The master said, Use what!

The master addressed the assembly, Before the world existed, this nature already existed; when the world is destroyed, this nature is not destroyed.
A monk asked, What is this nature? The master said, The five skandhas and four great elements. The monk said, Those still perish; what then is
this nature? The master said, The four great elements and five skandhas.

A scriptural lecturer from Dingzhou arrived. The master asked, What discipline do you study? He said, Sutras, vinaya, and treatises; I lecture without even listening first.
The master raised his hand and showed it to him, Can you lecture on this? The lecturer was bewildered and did not know. The master said, Even if
you can lecture without listening, you are still only a fellow who lectures on sutras and treatises. If it is Buddha-Dharma, it is not yet
there. He said, Then is not your present speech itself Buddha-Dharma? The master said, Even if you can ask
and answer, it all belongs to sutras and treatises; Buddha-Dharma is not there. He had no reply.

The master asked an attendant, Where do you come from? He said, From the north hall. The master said, How is that hall
compared with this hall? The attendant had no reply. A monk was standing nearby; the master told him to answer in the attendant's place, and the monk answered for him,
saying, I came from that hall. The master laughed at him. The master then told Wenyuan to answer for him, and Wenyuan said, The attendant
is still fine; he does not take the master's words.

The master asked a lecturer, What do you study? He said, I lecture on the Vimalakīrti Sutra. The master said, The Vimalakīrti Sutra says, Step by step
is the site of awakening. Where is the lecturer? No reply. The master told Quanyi to answer in the lecturer's place. Quan-
yi said, From this one question alone, can you recognize the site of awakening? The master said, Your body is in the site of awakening, your mind
is where? Speak quickly! He said, master is not seeking the student's mind. The master said, I am. He said,
This one question and one answer, what are they? The master said, This old monk is not in the mental factors; the Dharma passes beyond
eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind, and is known and understood. He said, Since it is not among the mind's reckonings, why does master
seek it? The master said, Because for your sake I cannot say it. He said, If the Dharma passes beyond eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind and is not
understood, how can you say it cannot be said? The master said, Eat my phlegm and spittle.

The master asked a monk, Have you ever read the Lotus Sutra? He said, I have. The master said, In the sutra it says, Wearing rag robes
in a secluded place, falsely called araṇya, one deceives and deludes worldly people. How do you understand this? The monk was about to
bow. The master said, Are you wearing rag robes? He said, I am wearing them. The master said, Do not deceive me. He said, How
can I avoid being deluded? The master said, Make your own living; do not take this old monk's words.

The master asked a lecturer, What do you study? He said, I lecture on the Vimalakīrti Sutra. The master said, Which one is Vimalakīrti's
grandfather? He said, I am. The master said, Then why are you passing on words for children and grandchildren? He had no reply.

One day the master ascended the hall. As soon as a monk came forward to bow, the master put his palms together and said, Take care. On another day a monk
bowed. The master said, Ask well. He said, What is Chan? The master said, Today the sky is cloudy; I will not answer
questions.

He asked a newly arrived monk, From what direction do you come? He said, I come from no direction. The master then turned his back. The monk took his sitting cloth
and turned along with the master. The master said, Fine indeed, no direction.

He asked a newly arrived monk, Where do you come from? He said, From the south. The master said, After meeting from three thousand li away, do not
play games. He said, I have not. The master said, Pluck willow blossoms, pluck willow blossoms.

Fenggan arrived below Mount Wutai and saw an old man. Fenggan said, Could you be Mañjuśrī or not? The old
man said, There cannot be two Mañjuśrīs. Fenggan then bowed, and the old man disappeared. A monk cited this
to the master. The master said, Fenggan had only one eye. The master then ordered Wenyuan to play the old man, and I
will play Fenggan. The master said, Could you be Mañjuśrī or not? There could not be two Mañjuśrīs. The master said, Man-
juśrī, Mañjuśrī.

The master asked two newly arrived monks, Reverend, have you been here before? One said, I have not been here. The master said, Go drink tea.
He then asked the other one, Have you been here before? He said, I have. The master said, Go drink tea. The prior
of the monastery asked, master, telling one who has not been here to go drink tea is one thing; but one who has been here, why
do you also tell him to go drink tea? The master said, Prior. The prior answered, Yes. The master said, Go drink tea.

The master went to Yunju. Yunju said, You are old indeed; why not find a place to stay? The master said, What
place can one stay in? Yunju said, Ahead there is the foundation of an old temple. The master said, Then, master,
find a place to stay yourself." The master again went to Zhuyu. Zhuyu said, "So old already, why not find a place to stay
and go there?" The master said, "What place can one stay in?" Zhuyu said, "So old already, and you do not even know a place to
stay!" The master said, "For thirty years I handled horses and rode them; today I was kicked down by a donkey." The master again went to Zhuyu's
abbot's room and looked all around. Zhuyu said, "Why take a fall on level ground?" The master said, "Only because
my mind is coarse."

One day the master took his staff up into Zhuyu's dharma hall, pacing east and west. Zhuyu said, "What are you
doing?" The master said, "Testing the water." Zhuyu said, "There is not a single drop here at my place, so what water are you testing?" The master then
leaned his staff against the wall and went down.

On the road to Mount Tai there was an old woman who liked to question monks. A monk asked:
"Which way does the road to Mount Tai go?" She said, "Go straight on!" As soon as the monk started off, the old woman said, "There goes another one like that
too!" After hearing of this, the master went and asked, "Which way does the road to Mount Tai go?" She said, "Go straight on!"
As soon as the master started off, the old woman said, "There goes another one like that too!" The master returned and told the assembly, saying, "Today that old woman
was seen through by this old monk."

Seeing a monk come, the master picked up a burning ember and showed it to him, saying, "Do you understand?" The monk said, "I do not." The master said, "You must not
call it fire; this old monk has said enough." The master again picked up the ember and said, "Do you understand?" He said, "I do not." The master then
said, "From here in Shu Prefecture there is the monk of Mount Touzi. Go pay respects and ask him. If the conditions accord,
you need not come back; if they do not accord, then come back." The monk then went, and upon arriving at Master Touzi's place,
Touzi asked, "Where have you just come from?" He said, "I left Zhaozhou and came especially to pay respects to you, Master!"
Touzi said, "What words did old Zhaozhou speak?" The monk then fully recounted the previous exchange. Touzi then
got down from the meditation seat, walked three or five steps, sat back down, and said, "Do you understand?" The monk said, "I do not!" Touzi said, "Go back and
tell Zhaozhou." The monk returned and told the master. The master said, "Do you understand yet?" He said, "Not yet."
The master said, "That is not much different either."

Dongshan asked a monk, "Where do you come from?" He said, "From mending shoes." Dongshan said, "By your own understanding, or relying on another?" He said, "Relying on another."
Dongshan said, "Did he also point to you, Venerable?" There was no reply. The master spoke on his behalf, saying, "If he approves, then it is not contrary."
Puhua was eating raw greens. Linji saw him and said, "Puhua is very much like a donkey." Puhua then
brayed like a donkey. Linji let it go and left. Puhua said, "That brat Linji has only one eye."
The master spoke on his behalf, saying, "Just give him his proper fodder."

Baoshou asked Hu Dingjiao, "Are you not Hu Dingjiao?" He said, "I would not dare." Bao said, "Can you nail
empty space?" He said, "Please first break open empty space!" Baoshou then struck him and said, "Later there will be a talkative master
who will explain it to you." Later Hu Dingjiao recounted this to the master. The master said, "Why were you
struck by him?" He said, "I do not know where my fault was!" The master said, "You cannot even manage this one seam,
and you still tell him to break it open!" Dingjiao then understood. The master spoke on his behalf, saying, "First nail up this one seam."
The master asked a newly arrived monk, "Where have you come from?" He said, "From Xuefeng." The master said, "What sayings does Xuefeng
use to instruct people?" He said, "Xuefeng often says, 'The whole world in the ten directions is the monk's
single eye. You people, where will you shit?' " The master said, "When you return, send Venerable Xuefeng a
shovel for me."

While traveling on the road, the master met an old woman who asked, "Where does the master live?" The master said, "Zhaozhou
East Courtyard west." The master later raised this to a monk, saying, "Tell me, which west character is used there?" One monk said, "The west of east-west."
Another monk said, "The west of dwelling and lodging." The master said, "You two could both serve as magistrates of the salt and iron office."

The master was strolling in the garden with a vice minister. They saw a rabbit run past, and the minister asked, "Master, you are a great spiritual teacher;
why did the rabbit run when it saw you?" The master said, "This old monk likes to kill."

The master saw a monk sweeping the ground and asked, "Sweeping like this, can it ever become clean?" He said, "The more I sweep,
the more there is." The master said, "Is there not one who brushes away dust?" He said, "Who is the one who brushes away dust?" The master said, "Do you understand?"
He said, "I do not." The master said, "Go ask Yunju." The monk then went and asked Yunju, "Who
is the one who brushes away dust?" Yunju said, "That blind fellow."

The master asked a monk, "How long have you been here?" He said, "Seven or eight years." The master said, "Have you ever seen this old
monk?" He said, "Yes." The master said, "If I were a donkey, how would you see me?" He said, "I would see in the dharma realm."
The master said, "I thought you had this move; all those meals you ate were wasted!" The monk said,
"Please speak, Master." The master said, "Why did you not say, 'See in the fodder'?"

The master asked the cook, "Today are we eating raw vegetables or cooked vegetables?" The cook lifted a stalk of greens. The master said,
"Those who know gratitude are few; those who betray gratitude are many."

A lay practitioner came to the monastery to burn incense. The master asked a monk, "He is over there burning incense and bowing, while I
am here talking with you. At just such a time, where is life?" The monk said, "Master,
what is it?" The master said, "In that case it is over there." He said, "In that case, that is already prior." The master
laughed at him.

The master and his junior attendant Wenyuan debated doctrine; neither could claim victory. Whoever claimed victory had to lose a pancake. The master said,
"I have a donkey!" Yuan said, "I am the donkey's rump." The master said, "I am donkey dung." Yuan said, "I
am a worm in the dung." The master said, "What are you doing in there?" Yuan said, "I am spending the summer there."
The master said, "Bring the pancake here."

As the master was returning from going inside, he saw a banner pole missing one section. A monk asked, "One section of the banner pole
is gone; did it go up to heaven or into the earth?" The master said, "It neither went to heaven nor entered the earth." He asked,
"Then where did it go?" The master said, "It fell off."

While the master was seated, a monk came out and bowed. The master said, "Take care." As the monk was about to ask a question, the master said, "Again
it is so."

While standing under the eaves, the master heard swallows chattering and said, "These swallows mutter on and on, enticing people into
talk." A monk asked, "I wonder, is it pleasant or not?" The master said, "Vaguely like a tune, just enough to listen to,
and then the wind blows it into another melody."

A monk took leave to depart. The master said, "Where are you going?" He said, "To Min." The master said, "There are many soldiers in Min;
you must avoid them." He said, "Where should I avoid them to?" The master said, "Just right."

A monk came for an interview and saw the master sitting with his patched robe covering his head, so he withdrew. The master said, "Venerable,
do not say this old monk did not respond."

The master asked a monk, "Where do you come from?" He said, "From the south." The master said, "Who was your companion?"
He said, "A water buffalo." The master said, "What a fine monk you are, but why keep company with a beast?" He said, "Because there is no difference."
The master said, "A fine beast indeed." He said, "How could I agree?" The master said, "If you do not agree, then give me back my companion
first."

The master asked a monk, "Is there a patriarch in the hall or not?" He said, "There is." The master said, "Call him to come wash
this old monk's feet."

In the hall there were two monks, each pushing the other away and refusing to be first seat. The steward reported this to the master. The master said,
"Make them both second seat." He asked, "Then who should be first seat?" The master said, "Set out the incense." He said,
"The incense has already been set out." The master said, "The incense of precepts, the incense of concentration."

The master asked a monk, "Where have you come from?" He said, "From the capital." The master said, "Did you pass through Tong Pass?"
He said, "I did not pass through it." The master said, "Today I have caught a smuggler of illicit salt."

While seeing off a deceased monk, the master said, "It is only one dead man, yet countless people escort him." He also said, "So many
dead men escort one living man." At that time a monk asked, "Is it the mind that lives, or the body that lives?" The master said,
"Body and mind both do not live." He said, "Then what is this?" The master said, "A dead fellow."

A monk saw a cat and asked, "I call it a cat; what does the master call it,
I wonder?" The master said, "It is you who call it a cat."

When the king of Zhenzhou came to visit the master, the attendant came to report, saying, "The king has come." The master said, "May the king
have ten thousand blessings." The attendant said, "He is not here yet; he has only reached below the gate." The master said, "And yet you say the king has
come."

From atop the east privy he called for Wenyuan, and Wenyuan answered. The master said, "Up on the east privy, I cannot speak
Buddhist teaching with you."

While passing through the hall, he called the attendant, and the attendant answered. The master said, "What fine hall-merit."
The attendant had no reply.

When the master went to Linji's place, he had just begun washing his feet when Linji asked, "What is the meaning of the Patriarch's coming from the West?"
The master said, "I am right in the middle of washing my feet." Linji came closer and leaned over to listen. The master said, "If you understand, then understand.
If you do not, do not peck at it any further, what are you doing?" Linji flicked his sleeves and left. The master said, "After thirty years of pilgrimage,
today I have put a mistaken note under someone's foot."

When the master reached Guoqing Monastery on Tiantai and saw Hanshan and Shide, he said, "Long have I heard of Hanshan and Shide;
coming here, I see only two water buffaloes." Hanshan and Shide then acted out buffalo fighting. The master said,
"Hey, hey!" Hanshan and Shide gnashed their teeth and glared at each other. The master returned to the hall. The two came into the hall and asked
the master, "How about that earlier exchange?" The master just laughed loudly.

One day the two asked the master, "Where have you been?" The master said, "To bow to the five hundred arhats." The two
said, "Five hundred water buffaloes, and where are the arhats?" The master said, "Why did they become five hundred
water buffaloes?" Hanshan said, "Blue heaven, blue heaven!" The master laughed loudly.

While on pilgrimage, the master met two hermitage masters. One was acting as a horn-haired page boy. The master greeted them, but the two
paid no attention at all. The next morning, the horn-haired boy brought a pot of rice, set it on the ground, and divided it into three
portions. The hermitage master pulled his mat forward and sat. The boy also pulled his mat forward and sat facing him,
still not inviting the master. The master then also pulled his mat forward and sat. The boy looked at the master, and the hermitage
master said, "Do not say you rose early; there are travelers by night as well." The master said, "Why not have this traveler
instructed?" The hermitage master said, "He is someone's son." The master said, "I should have let it pass." The boy then stood up,
looked at the hermitage master, and said, "Why so much talk?" From then on the boy entered the mountains and was not seen again.

While the master was reading a sutra, the novice Wenyuan came in. The master glanced at him from the side over the sutra. The novice
then went out. The master followed and seized him, saying, "Speak quickly! Speak quickly!" Wenyuan said, "Amitabha!
Amitabha!" The master then returned to the abbot's quarters.

When a novice boy came for interview, the master said to the attendant, "Tell him to go." The attendant told the boy,
"The master says to go." The master said, "The novice boy gets in through the gate; the attendant stays outside the gate."

While traveling, the master came to the monastery of a senior master. As soon as he entered and they saw each other, he said, "Is there? Is there?"
The senior master raised his fist. The master said, "The water is shallow; the boat is hard to moor." He then went out. He went to another monastery,
saw the senior master, and said, "Is there? Is there?" The senior master raised his fist. The master said, "Able to let go and able to seize,
able to take and able to gather." He bowed and went out.

One day the master held up a string of beads and asked a Silla elder, "Do they also have this over there?" He said,
"They do." The master said, "How is it compared with this?" He said, "It is not like this." The master said, "Since they have it, why is it not
like this?" There was no reply. The master answered for him, saying, "Have you not heard: Silla and Great Tang."

He asked a newly arrived monk, "Where do you come from?" He said, "From the south!" The master raised a finger and said, "Do you understand?" He said, "I do not."
The master said, "Ten thousand blessings in movement and stillness. You do not understand?"

While on pilgrimage, the master asked Daci, "What is prajna's substance?" Daci said, "What is prajna's
substance?" The master laughed loudly and left. The next day Daci saw the master sweeping and asked, "What is prajna's
substance?" The master put down the broom, laughed loudly, and went away. Daci then returned to his
quarters.

The master came to Baizhang. Baizhang asked, "Where do you come from?" He said, "From Nanquan." Baizhang said, "What
sayings does Nanquan use to instruct people?" The master said, "Sometimes he says, 'Even one who has not attained must still stand steeply
forth.' " Baizhang shouted at him. The master's expression turned startled. Baizhang said, "Excellent, steep indeed." The master then danced
and went out.

The master came to Touzi's place and sat facing him at the meal. Touzi gave him a steamed cake to eat. The master said, "I will not eat."
Before long, griddle cakes were served, and Touzi had a novice pass one to the master. The master received the cake and then bowed to the novice
three times. Touzi remained silent.

A monk painted the master's portrait and presented it to him. The master said, "If it looks like this old monk, then beat me to death; if it does not look like me,
then burn it."

As the master was walking with Wenyuan, he pointed with his hand to a patch of ground and said, "This would be a fine place to set up a
patrol post." Wenyuan went and stood there, saying, "Bring your official pass!" The master then gave him
a slap. Yuan said, "The pass was clearly in order."

The master asked a newly arrived monk, "Where have you just come from?" He said, "Mount Tai." The master said, "Did you see Manjusri there or not?" The monk
spread out his hands. The master said, "Many can spread out their hands, but who has seen Manjusri?" He said, "Just guarding the breath in a murderous hurry." The master
He said, If one does not see the geese in the clouds, how would one know the cold of the desert frontier.

A monk asked, I have come from far away to throw myself on the Master; I ask the Master to receive me. The Master said, Under Sun Bin, why drill a tortoise shell?
The monk flicked his sleeves and went out. The Master said, I had taken him for a worthy one, but he has broken both his legs.

The Master and the head monk were looking at the stone bridge. He then asked the head monk, Who built this? He said, Li Ying built it.
The Master said, When he built it, where did he set his hand to begin? There was no reply. The Master said, You ordinarily speak of the stone bridge, but when asked
about the place where the work began, you do not even know.

The head monk of the Silla Monastery invited the Master for a meal. When the Master reached the gate, he asked, What monastery is this? He said, Silla
Monastery. The Master said, Between you and me lies an ocean.

The Master asked a monk, Where do you come from? He said, From Yunju. The Master said, What saying does Yunju have? He said:
A monk asked, What is it like when the spirit ram hangs its horns? Yunju said, Six sixes are thirty-six. The Master said, Yun
ju, elder brother teacher, is still around. The monk then asked, I do not yet know Your Reverence's meaning. The Master said, Nine nines
are eighty-one.

One evening an old woman came into the monastery. The Master said, What are you doing? The old woman said, Seeking lodging. The Master said:
What kind of place is this here? The old woman laughed heartily and left.

The Master went out and met an old woman carrying a basket. The Master then asked, Where are you
going? She said, Going to steal Zhaozhou's bamboo shoots. The Master said, If you suddenly met Zhaozhou, then what? The old woman stepped close
and slapped him once.

The Master saw the monastery head bringing uncooked rice; when the crows saw him, they all flew off. The Master said, When the crows saw
you, why did they fly away? The head monk said, They fear me. The Master said, What kind of talk is that?
The Master answered in his place: Because I have a mind to kill.

The Master asked a monk, Where do you come from? He said, From Jiangxi. The Master said, Where is Zhaozhou located?
The monk had no reply.

The Master was passing by the hall and saw a monk bowing. The Master struck him once with a staff and said, Bowing is also a good
thing. The Master said, A good thing is not as good as no thing.

When the Master visited Tong Pass, the guard at Tong Pass asked him, Do you know there is a Tong Pass or not? The Master said, I know
there is a Tong Pass. He said, Those with official papers may pass; those without official papers may not pass. The Master said:
If the imperial carriage were suddenly to arrive, what then? The guard said, It too must be inspected before passing. He said, You want to rebel.
The Master came to Baoshou. When Baoshou saw him coming, he turned his back and sat facing away. The Master then spread out his sitting cloth.
Baoshou stood up, and the Master immediately went out.

When the Master was with Nanquan, Nanquan led a water buffalo into the monks' hall and made a circuit around the hall.
The head monk then slapped the buffalo's back three times, and Nanquan stopped and left. Later the Master placed a bundle of grass
before the head monk, and the head monk had no reply.

A scholar saw the Master and praised him, saying, Reverend, you are an ancient Buddha. The Master said, Scholar, you are
a new Tathagata.

A monk asked, What is nirvana? The Master said, My hearing is poor. The monk asked again. The Master said, I am not
bothered by deaf ears. Then he had a verse: Broad and easy is the Great Way, nirvana's gate is right before your face; just sit, thinking without
limit, and next year spring will be spring again.

A monk asked, Are the two roads of life and death the same or different? The Master then had a verse: A wayfarer asks of life and death,
how can life and death be discussed? In Shuanglin, one pond of water; a bright moon shines over heaven and earth. To call that understanding upon the words,
this is to toy with spirit and soul. If you wish to grasp life and death, it is like a madman speaking dream-spring.

A monk asked, When the Buddhas face trouble, they hide their bodies in flames; when the Reverend faces trouble, where
do you hide your body? The Master then had a verse: He says the Buddha has trouble; I say he has disaster. Just watch how I avoid
trouble, where is there any following along? Being and nonbeing are not the point, going and coming are not going and coming. For your sake I speak of the difficult
Dharma, but face to face, have you recognized it yet?

lg.l:
At cockcrow, the Chou hour, sorrow comes when I get up and still look shabby. No skirt, no short jacket at all, only the form of a kasaya
barely remains. The loincloth has no waist, the trousers no opening, and on my head are three or five pecks of bluish ash. Looking north, I meant to practice
and benefit people, but who knew I would end up in such a wretched state.

At dawn, the Yin hour, this ruined monastery in a desolate village is hard to speak of. For the morning gruel there is not a grain of rice, only facing empty
windows and dust in the cracks. Only sparrows chatter, no one draws near; sitting alone, I often hear falling leaves. Who says that leaving home
cuts off love and hate? Thinking on it, without noticing, tears soak my cloth.

At sunrise, the Mao hour, purity itself turns into vexation. Conditioned merit is covered by dusty curtains, and endless fields
have never been swept. Much furrowing of brows, little to the heart's liking; unbearable are that black and yellow old ones from the east village. Offerings and support
they never bring, yet they let their donkeys eat the grass before my hall.

At mealtime, the Chen hour, I vainly look to the neighboring houses for cooking smoke. I parted from steamed buns years ago; today I can only
think and swallow saliva. Little recitation, frequent sighing; among a hundred households there is not one good person. Those who come only
say they want tea to drink, and if they get no tea they gulp and go off angry.

By midmorning, the Si hour, who knew shaving the head would come to this? For no reason I was invited to be a village monk, humiliated and hungry,
wretched, almost to death. Hu Zhangsan and Black Li Si never produce the least bit of respect. They just now suddenly
come to the gate only to ask to borrow tea and borrow paper.

At noon, the Wu hour, tea and meals come around with no fixed measure. I leave the south house and go to the north house, and once I get to the north
house they make excuses. Bitter sandy salt, barley vinegar, sorghum rice with pickled lettuce. They only call this an offering
not to be taken lightly, and say a monk's resolve on the Way must be firm.

In the afternoon, the Wei hour, this time I do not trample the ground of passing time. I once heard one full meal makes one forget a hundred hungers; today this old
monk's body itself proves it. I practice no meditation, discuss no doctrine, spread out a torn mat and sleep in the sun. I reckon that even in
Tusita Heaven above, there is nothing like this sun baking my back.

In the late afternoon, the Shen hour, there are also people who burn incense and bow. Five old women, three with goiters, and one pair of faces
black and rough. Sesame tea is truly a treasure. Vajra, no need to strain so hard. I only wish that next year,
when silkworms and wheat are ripe, little Rahula will give me a coin.

At sunset, the You hour, apart from desolation what is there to keep? Of lofty wandering monks, surely none are entrusted here; temple-roaming novices
are always around. Extraordinary words never reach the mouth, and in vain I continue as a descendant of Shakyamuni. One staff,
rough and thorny, serves not only for climbing mountains but also for beating dogs.

At dusk, the Xu hour, I sit alone in an empty dark room. I never encounter lamp-light bright as sunlight; before my eyes there is only
Jinzhou lacquer-blackness. I hear no bell, the days pass in vain; only the mice I hear chattering and squeaking. By what means could I still
have the mood to think of reciting a paramita?

In the night watch, the Hai hour, who loves the bright moon before the gate? Here I worry only that when I lie down, there is no clothing
to use for a cover. Liu the steward, Zhao of the five precepts, what strange goodness they speak with their mouths. Let you mountain monks
have your bags utterly empty; if asked about it, all because you simply do not understand.

At midnight, the Zi hour, when have mind and circumstances ever paused even briefly? I think of all the home-leavers under heaven; among abbots like me,
how many can there be? An earthen couch-bed, broken reed mat, an old elm-wood pillow, and no blanket at all. Before the honored image I do not burn
benzoin incense; in the ashes there is only the smell of cow dung.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Seeing a pagoda being built, he had a verse
cb:div.head: Seeing a pagoda being built, he had a verse

lg.l:
Originally complete and perfect, what need is there to pile up stones? Names, distant and carved, are far removed from me. If someone asks,
I will never point it out or depict it.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Seeing that the understandings of various places went by different roads, he made a reproving verse
cb:div.head: Seeing that the understandings of various places went by different roads, he made a reproving verse

lg.l:
South of Zhaozhou, north of the stone bridge, in Guanyin Monastery there is Maitreya. The Patriarch left behind a single sandal,
and to this very day it cannot be found.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Because of the fish drum, he had a verse
cb:div.head: Because of the fish drum, he had a verse

lg.l:
The four great elements are indeed nature's work; if it has sound, what is most precious is the emptiness inside. Do not wonder that I do not speak with ordinary people
about it, only because the pitch and mode are not the same.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Because of a lotus, he had a verse
cb:div.head: Because of a lotus, he had a verse

lg.l:
A wondrous root and shoot, fresh with snow; no one knows in what age it left the Western Heaven. The depth or shallowness of the muddy mire people do not
know; only when it rises from the water do they know it is a white lotus.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Recorded Sayings of Reverend Zhaozhou, Lower ScrollEnd.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Biographical Record of Chan Master Zhenji of Zhaozhou
cb:div.head: Biographical Record of Chan Master Zhenji of Zhaozhou
cb:div.p:
The Master was a disciple in Nanquan's lineage. His secular surname was Hao, originally from Hao Village in Caozhou; his personal name was Cong
shen. A pagoda inscription in Zhenfu says: Did the Master reach seven hundred jiazi cycles? When King Wu was in decline, he withdrew to avoid
the times in remote mountains, eating roots and wearing grass, yet the deportment of a monk did not change. At first the Master traveled with his original teacher and came to Nan
quan. His original teacher first completed the formal greetings; only then did the Master make his greeting. Nanquan was lying in the abbot's room,
and when he saw the Master come to visit, he asked, From where have you lately come? The Master said, Ruixiang Monastery. Nanquan said, Did you
see the auspicious image? The Master said, The auspicious image I did not see, but I did see a reclining Tathagata. Nanquan then got up
and asked, Are you a novice with a master, or a novice without a master? The Master replied, A novice with a master. Nanquan said:
Which one is your master? The Master said, Though it is early spring and still cold, I respectfully wish Your Reverence's honored body peace and ten thousand
blessings. Nanquan then called the steward and said, Arrange another place for this novice. After the Master received full ordination, he heard that his ordination
teacher was west of Caozhou, residing at Huguo Monastery, so he returned there to pay respects. After he arrived, his original teacher ordered
the Hao family, saying: Your son has returned from wandering among teachers. His relatives were overjoyed beyond measure and only
waited for the next day, all planning to come see him. When the Master heard this, he said: The dusty net of worldly affection has no end.
I have already left home; I do not wish to meet them again. That very night he packed up and went on ahead. Afterward he himself carried
his bottle and staff and traveled everywhere. He often said of himself: If a seven-year-old child surpasses me, I will ask
him; if a hundred-year-old old man is not equal to me, I will teach him. Not until he was eighty did he settle at Zhao
zhou, at Guanyin Monastery east of the city, ten li from the stone bridge. Thereafter his abbacy was austere and spare, his resolve was to emulate the ancients.
In the monks' hall there were no front or rear platforms; meals were arranged only as needed. One leg of his rope bed was broken, so he used a burnt piece of firewood
and tied it on with rope. Whenever someone made a new one for him, the Master would not permit it. For more than forty years as abbot,
he never once sent a single letter to solicit his patrons. Then a monk from the south came and quoted a question to Xuefeng:
What is it like when an ancient ravine has a cold spring? Xuefeng said, Stare with wide eyes and you cannot see the bottom. The student said, For one who drinks it, how
is it? Xuefeng said, It does not enter through the mouth. When the Master heard this, he said: If not through the mouth, then it enters through the nostrils.
That monk then asked the Master, What is it like when an ancient ravine has a cold spring? The Master said, Bitter. The student said, For one who drinks it, how
is it? The Master said, Dead. When Xuefeng heard the Master's words, he praised him, saying: Ancient Buddha, ancient Buddha. Because of this, Xuefeng
afterward no longer answered such questions. Later, when the King of Yan in Hebei led troops to take Zhenfu, once they reached the border
a reader of omens reported: In Zhaozhou there dwells a sage; if you fight, you will surely not win. Yan
and Zhao, the two kings, thereupon spread a banquet and both ceased hostilities. Then they asked: In Zhao's golden land, what superior man
is there? Someone said: There is a great master who lectures on the Avatamsaka Sutra, lofty and solitary in conduct. In years of great drought, all command him
to go to Mount Tai to pray. Before the master returns, sweet rain pours down. They said: I fear this is not yet the very best. Someone
said: One hundred twenty li from here is Zhaozhou Guanyin Monastery. There is a Chan master there, advanced in years and seniority,
with clear Dharma eye. All said: This one may fit the omen. The two kings halted their carriages and went to see him. Once they arrived
in the monastery, the Master sat upright and did not rise. The King of Yan then asked: Which is more honored, the king of men or the Dharma King?
The Master said: If among kings of men, the king of men is honored; if among Dharma kings, the Dharma King is honored. Yan
King assented. After a while the Master asked: Which one is the great king of Zhenfu? The King of Zhao
responded: Your disciple. The Master said: This old monk idly remains in the mountains and rivers and has not been ableBecause Zhaozhou was affiliated with the prefectural government, this was done to express due respect.
to come face you. After a moment, attendants asked the Master to teach Dharma for the great king. The Master said: The great king has many attendants,
how can they let this old monk speak Dharma? So they ordered the attendants to withdraw. At the Master's side there was a novice, Wen
yuan, who said loudly: Inform the great king, these are not the attendants in question. The great king then asked: What
attendants are those? He replied: The great king has many taboo names, so the Reverend does not dare speak Dharma. The King of Yan then
said: I ask the Chan master to set aside the taboos and teach Dharma. The Master said: From this I know that the great king's relatives in former kalpas were all
enemies. Our Buddha, the World-Honored One, when his name is spoken once, sins are extinguished and blessings arise. But your royal ancestors, as soon as someone
touches upon their names, anger arises. The Master's compassion did not tire; he taught Dharma for a long time. The two kings
bowed their heads and praised him, honoring him without end. The next day, when they were about to return, the King of Yan sent a vanguard envoy, who heard that the Master
did not rise, and at dawn entered the monastery to accuse the Master of arrogance toward the lords. When the Master heard this, he came out to greet him. The vanguard
then asked: Yesterday, when the two kings came, you did not rise; today, when you see me come, why
did you rise to greet me? The Master said: When the commander becomes equal to a great king, this old monk also will not rise to greet him. The vanguard, hearing
the Master's words, bowed again and again and left. Soon after, the King of Zhao sent messengers to invite the Master for offerings. When he arrived
at the city gate, the whole city in formal array received him and escorted him inside. As soon as the Master descended from the jeweled carriage, the king bowed and invited
the Master up to the hall, where he sat in the proper seat. After a while, the Master struck his forehead with his hand and said: Those standing below the steps, what
officials are they? The attendants said: They are honored elders of the various monasteries, along with great masters and senior worthies. The Master said: Each of them
is a teaching host in his own place. If they remain below the steps, this old monk will also rise. The king then ordered them up into the hall. That day
as the feast was about to end, the monastic officials lined up, and from top to bottom each person was to ask one question. One person asked about Buddha
Dharma. As soon as the Master saw him, he asked: What are you doing? He said: Asking about Buddha Dharma. The Master said: Here you have already seated
this old monk aside; where then are you asking what Dharma? Two honored ones do not teach side by side. The king then orderedThis is a word used in the wording.
it stopped. At that time the queen and the king were both standing in attendance at his sides. The queen said: I ask the Chan master to, for
The great king receives the prediction with a touch on the crown. The Master rubbed the great king's crown with his hand and said, May Your Majesty and this old monk
have the same span of years. At that time, when arrangements were made to receive the Master, he was temporarily lodged in a nearby monastery, and in due time a site was chosen to build a Chan monastery.
When the Master heard of it, he had someone tell the king, If a single blade of grass is disturbed, this old monk will return to Zhaozhou.
At that time, Commander Dou vowed to donate an orchard, worth fifteen thousand strings of cash, to be named Zhenji
Chan Monastery, also called the Dou Family Garden. After the Master entered the monastery, the assembly gathered like clouds from the sea. At that time the Prince of Zhao
offered reverential support, and the Prince of Yan sent from Youzhou ceremonial robes bestowed by decree. The prefectural authorities prepared full honors to receive him. The Master
firmly declined and would not accept them. Attendants carried the chest before the Master and said, For the sake of the Chan Master's Buddha Dharma, the great king
earnestly asks the Master to wear these robes. The Master said, It is precisely for the sake of the Buddha Dharma that this old monk does not wear these
robes. The attendants said, At least do it for the great king's sake. The Master said, What concern is it of worldly officials! Then he personally
took the robe and draped it on himself. They bowed and offered congratulations again and again, and the Master merely acknowledged them. The Master stayed in Zhaozhou
for two years. When he was about to leave the world, he told his disciples, After I pass away, cremate me, and do not
wash and sort the relics. The disciples of a lineage master are not like the superficial customs of the world. Besides, the body is an illusion, so what relics could there be
to produce? This must not be done. He ordered a young attendant monk to send one whisk to the Prince of Zhao, with the message: This
is what this old monk could not use up in a lifetime. On the tenth day of the eleventh month in the year of wuzi, the Master sat upright
and died. At that time, at the Dou Family Garden, there were tens of thousands of monks and laypeople with carts and horses, and their cries of grief shook
the plains and fields. The Prince of Zhao carried out all the funeral rites, weeping with sorrow and lamentation, no different from when the golden coffin was concealed
in splendor at Kusinagara. All raised a lofty wild-goose stupa and specially erected a grand stele, bestowing the posthumous title Zhenji
Chan Master, and Guangzu Stupa. On the thirteenth day of the first summer month in the eleventh year of Baoda of the Later Tang,
a student consulted Chan Master Huitong of the Eastern Monastery in the Eastern Capital about the causes and course of the former man's teaching activity in Zhaozhou.
He bowed and withdrew, then took up the brush and recorded it. It is fully factual.

p.note: "to gather; to restrain" (CB); "to gather; to restrain" (Jiaxing, variant form)
posthumous title (CB); posthumous title (Jiaxing, variant form)
